OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

Working retail here in New York, I see how people get fooled by these stories all the time, and it worries me sick. Just last week, someone came into the store upset about some health claim they'd seen on Facebook that turned out to be complete nonsense. As someone who's been through a lot in life, immigrating here and building a family with my partner, I've learned that trust is everything, and these fake posts shake that foundation.

My approach is pretty simple, though I'll admit my anxiety makes me overthink things. Whenever I see something that gets my heart racing—especially anything about health, politics, or family safety—I force myself to pause before sharing or believing it. I check if the source looks legitimate, and I'm not shy about calling people out when I spot obvious fabrications. My high extraversion means I'm comfortable speaking up in conversations, which actually helps because I can warn friends directly rather than stewing about it alone.

The thing is, I don't have a ton of formal education, so I'm honest about that. I can't always tell the difference between sophisticated deepfakes and reality, which makes me even more anxious. But I've learned to look for red flags—crazy headlines designed to provoke anger, sources I've never heard of, images that seem off somehow. I talk to my kids about it too, because they're growing up in this digital world and need to be smarter than I was at their age.

What really helps me is limiting my time on social media to just a few times a week. When I do scroll, I'm intentional about it. I follow accounts that share genuine hobbies like birdwatching and cooking tips rather than getting pulled into drama. I've unfollowed people who constantly share unverified nonsense because, honestly, my mental health can't handle that constant stress. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I'll be straight with you—dealing with fake content online gets under my skin more than it probably should. Working retail here in New York, I see how people get fooled by these stories all the time, and it worries me sick. Just last week, someone came into the store upset about some health claim they'd seen on Facebook that turned out to be complete nonsense. As someone who's been through a lot in life, immigrating here and building a family with my partner, I've learned that trust is everything, and these fake posts shake that foundation.

My approach is pretty simple, though I'll admit my anxiety makes me overthink things. Whenever I see something that gets my heart racing—especially anything about health, politics, or family safety—I force myself to pause before sharing or believing it. I check if the source looks legitimate, and I'm not shy about calling people out when I spot obvious fabrications. My high extraversion means I'm comfortable speaking up in conversations, which actually helps because I can warn friends directly rather than stewing about it alone.

The thing is, I don't have a ton of formal education, so I'm honest about that. I can't always tell the difference between sophisticated deepfakes and reality, which makes me even more anxious. But I've learned to look for red flags—crazy headlines designed to provoke anger, sources I've never heard of, images that seem off somehow. I talk to my kids about it too, because they're growing up in this digital world and need to be smarter than I was at their age.

What really helps me is limiting my time on social media to just a few times a week. When I do scroll, I'm intentional about it. I follow accounts that share genuine hobbies like birdwatching and cooking tips rather than getting pulled into drama. I've unfollowed people who constantly share unverified nonsense because, honestly, my mental health can't handle that constant stress. Living in New York, you'd think I'd be more cynical, but my faith reminds me that truth matters and that spreading lies hurts real people. So I do my part by being skeptical, asking questions, and not contributing to the problem.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona exhibits high extraversion (95) and high neuroticism (93), suggesting someone who is socially engaged but emotionally reactive and anxious. Low conscientiousness (29) indicates less systematic approach to information verification, while low agreeableness (18) suggests directness in confronting misinformation. As a 50-year-old retail worker with lower secondary education, the persona would approach fake content from a practical, lived-experience perspective rather than academic framework. Immigration background and family responsibilities (3 children) create personal stakes in understanding trustworthiness. Moderate openness (48) indicates balanced skepticism without excessive cynicism. Religious beliefs inform values around truth and community harm. Hobbies like birdwatching and reading suggest preference for authentic, focused content. Limited social media use (few times weekly) positions this person as someone who has already developed protective boundaries.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, conversational, emotionally honest, slightly anxious but pragmatic, socially engaged, concerned about community welfare, unpretentious, speaking from personal experience rather than expertise
